ontology.foodex2_description: Meat-based stew with legumes — Chili con Carne (beef and kidney bean stew in tomato-chili sauce)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Chile con Carne code; A0CKW (meat-based stew) is the closest defensible category anchor, representing a broader category that includes this dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for Chile con Carne identified in the clinical terminology; ontology coverage satisfied by FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chile con Carne weighs approximately 350 g, consistent with a typical restaurant or home-cooked serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chili con carne with beans, SR Legacy (FDC ID 172949)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Chili con carne with beans' (FDC ID 172949) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Input values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA data and found consistent within acceptable rounding tolerances. GrabFood is a forbidden primary source and is not cited as evidence. Sodium 257 mg/100g is slightly above the USDA reference (~220 mg/100g) and is retained as plausible for a restaurant-style preparation with added salt. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water, and several B-vitamins are populated from USDA SR Legacy composite data for chili con carne with beans.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Chile con Carne with beef exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 23 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: kidney beans, boiled (GI = 24, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). Kidney beans are the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × net available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 23 × (8.6 − 2.9) g/100g × 3.5 / 100 = 23 × 19.95 / 100 ≈ 4.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 257 mg/100g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium, representing 39% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request low-sodium preparation, avoid adding salt at the table, and limit portion size to 200 g to keep sodium intake below 520 mg per eating occasion; the potassium content (~320 mg/100g) from kidney beans and tomatoes provides a partial offsetting benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~23) driven by kidney beans; dietary fibre 2.9 g/100g slows glucose absorption; net carbohydrate 5.7 g/100g.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chile con Carne is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served without white rice or tortilla chips; the low GI and high fibre content support glycaemic control, but portion size should be limited to 250–300 g to manage total carbohydrate and caloric load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.3 g/100g and cholesterol 25.7 mg/100g from ground beef; a 350 g serving provides approximately 8 g saturated fat (40% of the 20 g daily limit for a 2,000 kcal diet).
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size to 200 g, choose lean ground beef (≤10% fat) or substitute with turkey mince to reduce saturated fat intake, and note that kidney beans and tomatoes contribute beneficial soluble fibre and lycopene that support vascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ground beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute ground beef with ground turkey or chicken to reduce arachidonic acid load; kidney beans, cumin, garlic, and tomatoes provide anti-inflammatory benefit if the meat is substituted, but nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers) should be monitored for individual tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chile con Carne (also spelled Chili con Carne) is a hearty, slow-simmered stew of Mexican-American origin, traditionally made with ground or diced beef, kidney beans, tomatoes, chili peppers, onion, garlic, and spices such as cumin and oregano. The dish is cooked by browning the beef, then simmering all ingredients together until the sauce thickens and the flavours meld, producing a rich, deeply savoury, mildly spicy stew with a robust tomato-chili base. The texture is thick and chunky, with tender meat and soft beans. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 128 kcal, 10 g protein, 5.7 g fat (2.3 g saturated), 8.6 g carbohydrate, 2.9 g dietary fibre, and 257 mg sodium. The glycemic index is low (~23), driven by kidney beans, making it suitable for glycaemic management when served without high-GI accompaniments. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium content (~900 mg per bowl), moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from beef, and low concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI and high fibre. It is not recommended for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid content of red meat. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. It is commonly served with rice, tortilla chips, sour cream, or shredded cheese in restaurant settings.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chili con carne with beans (FDC ID 172949) and are consistent with the input record. The input provenance is ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood, which is a forbidden primary source; USDA data is used as the approved anchor. GI is estimated from analogous kidney bean entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because no exact Singapore-specific or restaurant-specific laboratory analysis is available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chili con carne with beans, FDC ID 172949) as the approved primary anchor. Input values from GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found consistent; GrabFood is not an approved primary source and is not cited as evidence. 2. Sodium value of 257 mg/100g is slightly above the USDA reference (~220 mg/100g) and is retained as plausible for a restaurant-style preparation with added salt. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1: detailed vitamin D data for this mixed stew preparation is not reliably reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for the chili con carne composite entry (FDC ID 172949); the field is set to -1 to indicate data unavailability rather than a confirmed zero. 4. GI of 23 is an analogous estimate based on kidney beans (GI = 24) from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283); no direct GI measurement for beef-and-bean chili exists in the database. 5. The input record listed 'country_of_origin: Singapore' and 'region: Southeast Asia', which reflects the GrabFood listing location rather than the dish's true culinary origin; the dish originates from Texas/Northern Mexico and is classified accordingly. 6. The input ethnic_tags included 'Naan' which is unrelated to Chile con Carne and has been removed. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CKW is used as the closest defensible category anchor for meat-based stew; no dedicated Chile con Carne code exists in FoodEx2. 8. Multilingual names provided for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as the dish is served in Singapore (GrabFood listing); these are transliterations and translations appropriate for a Singapore food database context. 9. No core macro fields are zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name 'Chile con Carne' is precise and unambiguous. Multilingual identity satisfied with Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names present.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (128.0), protein_g (10.0), fat_g (5.7), carbohydrate_g (8.6) all present and numeric. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172949 — an approved primary source. Source URL points directly to the FDC food detail page.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (2.9), sodium_mg (257.0), saturated_fat_g (2.3), sugar_g (1.7) all present as numeric values and supported by USDA SR Legacy data.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (23), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (4.6), and gi_source all present. GI source is University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) — an approved GI source. Analogous estimate basis documented in source_match_notes.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03311069 ('chili con carne') is a defensible exact match. FoodEx2 code A0CKW used as closest parent with gap note. SNOMED CT null with documented gap note. No invented codes.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is 1,147 characters, well above the 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (low), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes correctly reads 'Type 2 Diabetes'.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: Flat aliases array contains 14 entries covering English variants, Chinese script forms, romanized form, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi. All values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Alias consistency check passed.
